issues.body: Both jobs first run `python3 scripts/retriage.py --self-test`. When a regression makes that self-test fail, the retriage job becomes `failure`, but the alert job then fails on the identical self-test and never reaches the `--alert` step. Thus a scheduled retriage outage caused by a self-test failure produces no rolling issue even though the alert job is documented as catching job-level failures. The live-workflow assertion only proves that the alert step is unmasked; it does not prove the step is reachable when the upstream result is failure.
issues.fix_hint: Gate the alert path with tests that are independent of the sweep failure it is reporting (for example, a focused alert-only self-test), or otherwise ensure `--alert` still runs when the shared/full self-test fails while retaining fail-loud behavior for alert implementation failures. Add a non-vacuous workflow/self-test assertion covering an upstream self-test failure and proving the alert invocation remains reachable.
issues.title: The alert job is blocked by the same self-test failure it must report

summary: The round-2 malformed-lookup finding is correctly fixed: malformed and non-array JSON now return nonzero, avoid mutation and payload echoing, and the self-test asserts that behavior. However, the new alert job cannot report an important class of retriage failures: it reruns the exact same full retriage self-test that gates the retriage job before invoking --alert. If that self-test is what failed, the alert job fails at the same step and never creates or updates the rolling alert. This contradicts the stated job-level fail-loud guarantee. Because this is a new major finding, progress is regressing under the required rubric.
